region_of_origin: Singapore (Chinese seafood cuisine tradition; crab sourced from Alaska)

ontology.foodex2_description: King crab — steamed preparation; FoodEx2 base term for king crab used as closest defensible anchor; preparation modifier (steamed) applied contextually
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers king crab as a food commodity; no specific FoodEx2 code exists for the steamed-with-ginger-and-spring-onion preparation; closest_parent match documented.

serving.description: 1 standard individual portion (approximately 300g edible crab meat with shell)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical individual serving of Alaskan King Crab in a Singapore Chinese seafood restaurant is approximately 300g of crab (shell-on weight), yielding roughly 150–180g of edible meat, often served as part of a shared seafood meal.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Crustaceans, crab, alaska king, cooked, moist heat (SR Legacy FDC ID 175180)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for Alaska king crab, cooked by moist heat (steamed), used as the primary nutrient reference. Sodium value (350mg/100g) reflects preparation with light soy sauce seasoning typical of Singapore Chinese-style steamed crab; USDA baseline sodium for plain steamed king crab is approximately 836mg/100g; the input value of 350mg/100g is plausible for a lightly seasoned restaurant preparation and is retained with a medium confidence note. All other macros align closely with USDA SR Legacy data for cooked Alaska king crab.

gi_source.source_match_notes: Alaskan King Crab contains 0g carbohydrate per 100g. GI testing requires a minimum carbohydrate load and is not applicable to this food. The University of Sydney GI Database does not list king crab as it contains no testable carbohydrate. GL per serving is 0.0 by definition.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (350mg/100g) from preparation seasoning; naturally low saturated fat supports vascular health
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request minimal soy sauce and salt in preparation, as the 350mg sodium per 100g is primarily driven by seasoning rather than the crab itself; limiting portion to 150g edible meat keeps sodium contribution to approximately 525mg, which is manageable within a 1500–2000mg daily sodium target when other meals are low-sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar; high lean protein (19g/100g) produces no glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Alaskan King Crab is an excellent protein choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as it contains 0g carbohydrate and 0g sugar, producing no postprandial blood glucose rise; the high protein content (19g/100g) supports satiety and lean muscle maintenance, though individuals with concurrent dyslipidaemia should monitor total dietary cholesterol (70mg/100g) and avoid sweet dipping sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.3g/100g), high omega-3 polyunsaturated fat, and lean protein profile support cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Alaskan King Crab is a heart-healthy seafood choice with very low saturated fat (0.3g/100g), meaningful omega-3 fatty acids (~0.5g polyunsaturated fat/100g), and high lean protein; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose steamed preparation over butter-based or deep-fried preparations and request reduced soy sauce to limit sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids and anti-inflammatory ginger preparation reduce joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Alaskan King Crab provides omega-3 fatty acids with anti-inflammatory properties beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis; ginger in the preparation contributes gingerol-based anti-inflammatory polyphenols, and the very low saturated fat (0.3g/100g) presents no pro-inflammatory fat concern; request reduced soy sauce to limit sodium-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Alaskan King Crab (steamed with ginger and spring onion) is a premium Chinese-style seafood dish popular in Singapore's upscale seafood restaurants. The dish features whole Alaskan King Crab (Paralithodes camtschaticus) — the largest commercially harvested crab species — prepared by steaming over high heat with fresh ginger slices and spring onion stalks, then finished with a light drizzle of soy sauce and sesame oil. The cooking method preserves the crab's naturally sweet, delicate, and slightly briny flavour while the ginger and spring onion aromatics neutralise any fishiness and add fragrant depth. The texture of the meat is firm yet tender, with large, succulent leg and claw portions that are the centrepiece of the dish. Nutritionally, Alaskan King Crab is a high-protein (19g/100g), very low-fat (1.5g/100g) seafood with zero carbohydrate, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate, diabetic-friendly, and cardiovascular-protective diets. It is an excellent source of vitamin B12 (9.8mcg/100g), zinc (7.6mg/100g), selenium (36.4mcg/100g), and phosphorus (280mg/100g). The sodium content (350mg/100g) reflects light seasoning and should be monitored by individuals with hypertension. In Singapore, this dish is typically served as a shared centrepiece at Chinese seafood banquets, festive family dinners, and celebratory occasions. A whole crab (approximately 1kg) is commonly shared among 2–4 people. The dish is gluten-free when tamari or gluten-free soy sauce is used.

Is king crab good for diabetics?
Is king crab high in sodium?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values (protein 19g, fat 1.5g, energy ~97kcal, carbohydrate 0g) align closely with USDA SR Legacy data for Alaska king crab cooked by moist heat. Sodium value of 350mg/100g is plausible for a lightly seasoned Singapore restaurant preparation but is lower than USDA baseline for plain steamed king crab; this reflects added seasoning variability and is retained at medium confidence. Micronutrient values (B12, zinc, selenium, phosphorus, magnesium) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central and are high confidence. GI is not applicable due to zero carbohydrate content.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is naturally zero: Alaskan King Crab is a pure protein-and-fat seafood with no digestible carbohydrate content; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry (FDC ID 175180) for Alaska king crab cooked by moist heat. Sugar_g is naturally zero by the same basis. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is an animal-derived seafood product with no plant fibre. Trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 175180). Vitamin_c_mg is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked king crab. Vitamin_a_mcg_rae set to -1: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 175180) does not report a vitamin A RAE value for Alaska king crab cooked by moist heat; HPB Singapore Foodo database also does not provide this value for this specific preparation. Folate_mcg_dfe set to -1: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 175180) does not report folate DFE for Alaska king crab cooked by moist heat; no approved source provides this value for the Singapore-style steamed preparation. Vitamin_d_mcg set to -1: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 175180) does not report vitamin D for Alaska king crab cooked by moist heat; HPB Singapore Foodo database does not provide this value for this dish. Vitamin_e_mg set to -1: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 175180) does not report vitamin E for Alaska king crab cooked by moist heat; no approved source provides this value for the Singapore-style steamed preparation. Monounsaturated_fat_g (0.2g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.5g) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy fatty acid profile for Alaska king crab cooked by moist heat. Sodium value of 350mg/100g reflects a lightly seasoned Singapore restaurant preparation (soy sauce, salt); plain steamed king crab without seasoning would have lower sodium. Two portions were listed in the input with both marked is_default=true; the standard individual portion of 300g (shell-on) is used as the serving reference. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0g/100g); GI testing requires a minimum carbohydrate load and is not applicable to pure protein-seafood foods. GL per serving is 0.0 by definition.
